actions.thought: I am on the button with 8h 6d. This is a weak offsuit connector. While I have positional advantage, 86o is not strong enough to merit a raise in the first hand of the tournament against two opponents with full stacks. Standard play here is to fold and wait for better starting hand equity or a better opportunity to steal the blinds.

Queen-five offsuit is a weak, uncoordinated hand with poor equity in a heads-up scenario. As I am out of position in the small blind, playing this hand would likely lead to difficult decisions and lost chips. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for stronger starting hands.

I hold 5c 6d, which is a very weak starting hand with poor equity. Although I am in the button position, calling 100 into a 150 pot with such a weak hand is mathematically negative expected value. Given that this is only the second hand of the tournament, it is disciplined to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Folded to me in the Small Blind. Jack-Ten offsuit is a strong enough hand to open-raise to steal the Big Blind or play heads-up. Raising to 300 (3x) asserts the betting lead and puts pressure on the BB to defend.

I am in the Big Blind with 3h 4c. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I have position post-flop in this blind-vs-blind battle, 3h 4c is a very weak hand with poor equity and playability. Defending with such a marginal hand this early in the tournament is unnecessary. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

We are on the button with Q5o. This is a very weak offsuit hand. Raising as a steal with such weak holding is marginal at best against two opponents, and there's no need to force plays with garbage hands. I will fold and wait for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Ten suited (AhTh). After the Button (Pro) folded, it is a blind-vs-blind situation. Ace-Ten suited is a very strong starting hand in this scenario. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and exert pressure on the Big Blind, taking initiative in the hand.

Holding King-Deuce offsuit is a weak starting hand, especially out of position in the Big Blind against a raise from the Small Blind. Given the early stage of the tournament, it is better to conserve chips and wait for a stronger hand rather than playing a marginal holding that will be difficult to navigate post-flop.

62 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Although I am on the button, this hand has very little playability post-flop and poor equity against any reasonable calling range from the blinds. In a 3-handed game, I should be aggressive from the button, but 62o is too weak even for a wide opening range. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

4c 6h is a weak, unsuited hand with poor connectivity. As I am in the Small Blind and out of position for the rest of the hand against the Big Blind, there is little incentive to complete the small blind. I will fold to conserve my stack and wait for a more favorable spot early in the tournament.

With 3s and Jc, I have a very weak hand with poor connectivity and low high-card value. In a 3-handed game, while being on the button allows for a wider range, 3-J offsuit is not strong enough to warrant a raise or a limp. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal play to conserve my stack early in the tournament.

With A7o in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I have a strong hand for a blind-versus-blind situation. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) puts pressure on the Big Blind and often wins the pot uncontested. If called, I still have good high-card equity.

I am in the Big Blind with QsJh. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a blind-versus-blind situation, QJo is a strong hand that is well ahead of a typical SB opening range. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call (200 to win a pot that will be 600) and I will have the advantage of position for the remainder of the hand. Calling is the most standard and profitable play here to see a flop.

With top pair and a weak kicker on a wet and coordinated board (Ah Kh 2s), I want to make a small continuation bet. Betting 200 (1/3 pot) charges draws like flush draws and straight draws, gets value from worse made hands like Kx, and helps define my opponent's range while keeping the pot relatively controlled.

I have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (Broadway) on a board of 2s-Ah-Kh. The opponent (Pro) was the pre-flop aggressor and has led with a small continuation bet of 200 into a 600 pot. This bet size (1/3 pot) gives me immediate pot odds of 4-to-1 to call. Being in position with a strong draw and backdoor flush potential makes calling the most profitable decision, as it allows me to see a turn card cheaply and potentially capitalize on my draw if a Ten hits or evaluate if the opponent shows weakness on later streets.

The turn brings the Queen of Hearts, completing a very scary board with three hearts and three straight cards (Ah Kh Qh). Since I hold no hearts and only a weak kicker to my top pair (As 7d), my hand's relative value has plummeted. A bet here would likely only get called by better hands, such as flushes, straights, two pairs, or stronger Aces. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on Flash's action, though I'll likely have to fold to a substantial bet.

The turn card (Qh) improves my hand to a pair of Queens and gives me a flush draw with the Jack of Hearts. However, the board is now very dangerous as it contains three hearts and three cards to a straight (A-K-Q). Pro's check could be a sign of weakness, but it could also be a trap if they have already made a flush or a straight. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, see a free river card, and maintain the ability to realize my equity (hitting a heart for a flush or a Ten for the Nut straight) without risking a check-raise.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board. Holding no hearts, my top pair is essentially a bluff catcher, and a very weak one at that. Any single heart in Flash's hand makes a flush. Betting here serves no purpose, as worse hands will fold and better hands (any heart, straights, two pair) will call or raise. Checking is the only logical play, and I will be prepared to fold if facing a bet.

On the river, the board is 2s-Ah-Kh-Qh-3h. I hold the Jh, giving me an Ace-high flush (Ah-Kh-Qh-Jh-3h). Since the Ace, King, and Queen of hearts are all on the board, the Jack of hearts is the highest possible heart a player can hold. With no pairs on the board, a full house or four-of-a-kind is impossible, and no straight flush is possible given the board texture. Therefore, I have the absolute nuts. Pro has checked to me twice (on the turn and river). I need to bet for value to extract chips from any lower flush, a straight, or any other made hand Pro might be holding. A bet of 600 into the 1000 pot is a strong value bet that can still be called by weaker hands.

The river puts a fourth heart on the board. With no heart in my hand, my pair of Aces is purely a bluff-catcher against a very narrow range of total air. Flash's river bet of 600 into 1000 strongly represents at least a weak flush or better. Given the high likelihood that he holds any single heart, and since I block none of the important straight or flush combinations, folding is the only profitable long-term decision.

8h 9s is a weak offsuit connector/gapper. Being on the button 3-handed, while we have position, opening 89o is generally too loose and not profitable in the long run. Folding is the safest and standard play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Pro has already folded from the Button. While this is a blind-vs-blind situation, Queen-Trey offsuit is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability. Calling or raising here often leads to difficult situations out of position against the Big Blind. I will choose to fold and wait for a better hand to commit chips.

I am on the button with 9-3 offsuit. This is one of the weakest hands in the deck and does not warrant an open even from late position. While stealing blinds is important in 3-handed play, this specific hand has zero playability and very little equity if called. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the small blind with a premium holding, Ace-King suited (AdKd). Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. A raise is the standard and optimal play to build the pot and capitalize on my hand strength. I will raise to 300 chips (3BB) to apply pressure and control the hand.

Pocket Tens is a premium starting hand, especially heads-up against a Small Blind open. 3-betting extracts value from a wide SB opening range (smaller pocket pairs, broadways, suited connectors), takes the betting lead, and prevents my opponent from realizing cheap equity with random overcards.

I am in the small blind with a premium holding, Ace-King suited (AdKd). After my initial raise, the big blind (Pro) 3-bet to 900. Against a 3-bet, flat-calling out of position is suboptimal as it bloats the pot while sacrificing initiative. A 4-bet allows me to realize my equity and potentially take down the pot immediately or set up a favorable SPR for post-flop play. I will 4-bet to 2200 to assert dominance and build the pot with a strong range.

Facing a substantial 4-bet to 2200 out of the Small Blind, Pocket Tens becomes a marginal hand. While blind-versus-blind ranges are typically wider, a 4-bet of this size strongly indicates a premium range such as JJ+, AK, and occasionally AQs. We do not have the proper implied odds to purely set-mine given the stack sizes and the required 1300 call. Furthermore, flatting would leave us in a difficult situation post-flop with a low stack-to-pot ratio whenever overcards hit, which occurs on the majority of flops. A 5-bet shove would likely only be called by hands that dominate us. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined play to preserve our stack.

The starting hand 6d-4h is very weak and lacks potential to perform well against two opponents. Playing such trash hands, even on the button, is not profitable in the long run. The disciplined and optimal strategic decision is to fold and wait for a better hand to enter the pot.

98o is a very marginal hand out of position in the small blind. Although the button folded, attempting to steal or limp with a weak offsuit connector that plays poorly post-flop is unnecessarily risky. Folding is the standard, conservative play to preserve the stack.

I have Ace-Jack suited on the Button, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. I want to raise to take the initiative, build the pot, and either steal the blinds or play in position with a strong range. Raising to 300 is my standard opening size.

I have 89 suited in the small blind against a button open from Pro. This is a strong speculative hand with good post-flop playability. While I am out of position, the price to call is attractive (250 more into a 450 pot). Folding is too conservative, and 3-betting might be unnecessary given we are still early in the tournament. I will call to see a flop.

With J2o in the big blind against a raise and a call, I am getting decent immediate pot odds, but the hand has extremely poor equity and playability in a multi-way pot out of position. Given the early stage of the tournament, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for stronger starting hands where I have more leverage.

I have a gutshot straight draw on a board of 6-Q-T, needing a 7 to complete my straight (6-7-8-9-10). Since Pro was the pre-flop aggressor and this board texture (two broadway cards) connects well with a button opening range, I will check to the raiser to see their continuation bet sizing before deciding whether to proceed.

We have Ace-Jack of clubs on a 6d Qh Tc board. This gives us an overcard, a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (with a King), and a backdoor club flush draw. Our hand has excellent equity but minimal showdown value right now, making it a great candidate for a continuation bet. A bet of around half the pot should fold out underpairs to the Queen/Ten and random floats while allowing us to build the pot in case we hit our straight or flush draws.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 to make the nut straight) on a board of Qh-Tc-6d. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 350 into a pot of 700 (1/2 pot). I need to call 350 to potentially win a total pot of 1400, giving me 4:1 pot odds. My hand also has some backdoor potential with the two clubs in my hand and one on the board. While I am out of position, the implied odds of hitting the straight against a button-range continuation bet are high enough to justify a call here to see the turn.

On the turn, the 4 of hearts is a blank card that does not significantly change the board texture or improve my hand. I still have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to see what the opponent does. Betting here would likely be ineffective as a bluff and I do not have enough equity to bet for value.

The 4h on the turn doesn't improve our hand and brings in a flush draw, while our backdoor club draw is now dead. We still have a gutshot to the nut straight and an overcard. Betting again is unlikely to fold out hands like Qx or flush draws that Flash called with on the flop. Checking behind allows us to control the pot size, realize our equity for free, and re-evaluate on the river.

On the river, the board pairs the Ten and brings a potential heart flush (6d-Qh-Tc-4h-Th). I have 9-high and zero showdown value. However, the turn went check-check, which typically indicates that Pro (the pre-flop and flop aggressor) has a capped range, likely a single pair like a Queen or a marginal pocket pair, and is looking for a cheap showdown. My range, having called the flop, contains several Tens (T9s, JTs) and some heart draws that just completed. By leading out (a 'donk bet') on the river, I am representing either the trips or the flush. Given Pro's previous discipline in Hand #5 where they folded to river pressure, I will bet 700 (half the pot) to force a fold from their one-pair hands.

On the river, the board pairs the Ten and completes the front-door heart flush draw. We missed our gutshot straight draw and only have Ace-Jack high. Flash called our flop c-bet and is now leading out for half the pot. Given that Flash could easily have a Ten, a Queen, a flush, or a medium pocket pair that beats our Ace-high, and since we have no showdown value to speak of, we must fold our hand and preserve our stack.

I am on the button with Ten-Eight offsuit in a three-handed game. T8o is a standard opening candidate from this position to exert pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. Given my stack size and the current tournament dynamics, a 3x raise is a standard play to leverage my positional advantage.

K-6 offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the small blind, especially against a button open. Being out of position with a marginal holding makes it difficult to realize equity effectively post-flop. Folding is the disciplined play here to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger range.
Facing a raise from the Button, I hold 52o in the Big Blind. This is a clear fold due to it being one of the weakest starting hands.

Queen-three offsuit is an extremely weak hand, even in a three-handed game on the button. There is no strategic value in playing this hand, as it is dominated by most ranges and offers poor playability post-flop. The best course of action is to fold and conserve my stack for more profitable opportunities.
Q3o is a very weak hand. Playing it out of position against the big blind is not profitable. Folding is the standard and correct play.
Q8o is a weak offsuit hand. Even on the button 3-handed, it is not strong enough to open-raise profitably. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

With 6-8 offsuit in the Small Blind and the Button having folded, I am facing the Big Blind. 6-8 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and want to avoid unnecessary marginal spots against a potentially aggressive Big Blind, I will choose to fold rather than complete or raise.

I am on the Button with Ace-Five suited, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game. Opening with a raise is standard to seize initiative and use my positional advantage against the blinds. A 3x open to 300 is a consistent sizing for this game.

I am in the small blind with 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the position (out of position for the rest of the hand) and the strength of the hand, there is no mathematical justification to call the raise from the button. Folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack.
Ten-Six offsuit is a very weak hand to defend with from the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Button. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to preserve my stack.

I am dealt 5c3s on the button. This is an extremely weak, unplayable hand in a three-handed game. There is no strategic value in opening this hand or calling, so I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

8d 6d is a suited one-gapper with decent playability post-flop. With the Button folding, completing the small blind gives us a chance to see a cheap flop against the Big Blind while keeping the pot small since we will be out of position. Raising is also an option, but limping allows us to realize our equity without building a big pot out of position with a marginal hand.

I am in the Big Blind with 83o. The button folded and the Small Blind (Pro) just completed the bet. Since there is no additional cost to see the flop, I will check to see a free board and hope to improve my hand. Raising with such a weak hand into a limper is unnecessary at this stage of the tournament.

With middle pair and a backdoor flush draw, checking to pot-control is the standard play out of position on this dry board. Since the Big Blind checked pre-flop, they have a wide range, and we can comfortably evaluate their action. We don't want to build a big pot with a marginal hand.

I have absolutely no connection to this flop with 83o. The board is K-6-4 rainbow, and the SB has checked to me. While I could attempt to bluff, there's no reason to risk chips with such a weak hand in a small limped pot. I will check back to see a free turn card and likely fold to any future aggression unless I miraculously improve.

The 7s on the turn gives me a gutshot straight draw to go along with my middle pair of sixes. Since the flop checked through, it's unlikely my opponent has a strong King. Betting half the pot allows me to fold out random overcards that have equity against me, potentially extract value from weaker pairs or draws, and take control of the hand. If I face a raise, I can easily fold.

I have 8-high with a weak gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) on a K-6-4-7 board. Pro limped from the SB and checked the flop, then bet half-pot on the turn. While the price to call is small (100 into 300), 8-high has almost no showdown value, and my draw is to the low end of a straight. I've been playing conservatively, and there is no reason to start chasing thin draws in a limped pot with a hand this weak. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

6s 7s is a strong suited connector that plays very well in position. On the button in a 3-handed game, this is a prime hand to open with a raise to either steal the blinds immediately or play a pot with the positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 7h. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. While 6-7 are connectors, they are offsuit and low, making them difficult to play out of position against a button raiser. Calling would put me in the worst position for the rest of the hand with a marginal holding, and 3-betting as a bluff is less ideal with these specific cards compared to hands with better blockers. Therefore, the most prudent play to preserve my stack is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6 offsuit, a weak hand that plays poorly out of position against a button raise. Even though I am getting decent pot odds, continuing with such a weak hand against a wide opening range will frequently lead to difficult spots post-flop where I am dominated. Given the early stage of the tournament, folding is the most disciplined and +EV choice.

I am on the button with a premium starting hand, Ace-Queen suited. In a three-handed game, this hand is extremely strong. My goal is to open for a standard raise to build the pot and take control of the hand with positional advantage. A 3x raise to 300 is standard and effective here.

K7o is a weak hand to defend from the Small Blind against an aggressive Button opener. Playing this hand out of position makes it difficult to realize equity post-flop. Folding is the most disciplined choice to preserve my stack for a better spot.

Jack-Four offsuit is a very weak starting hand with low equity and almost no playability. Even though we are in the Big Blind and facing a button raise (which could be a steal attempt), defending with such a weak holding out of position is unprofitable. Folding is the best choice.

Qc-8h offsuit is a weak holding with limited playability and poor showdown value. In a 3-handed game, even on the button, this hand does not justify an investment of 100 chips. It is best to fold and wait for a stronger starting hand to enter the pot.

With King-Deuce offsuit from the small blind, the hand is too weak to play optimally out of position. Attempting to steal or limp with a disconnected hand that has a very poor kicker generally has negative expected value. Folding is the safest and most standard play here.
Holding 9c 7s, a weak offsuit two-gapper, on the Button. This is generally too weak to open raise or call with, even in position. The best action is to fold and wait for a better starting hand.

93o is an extremely weak hand to play from the small blind, even in a short-handed game. While Flashlight has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression in previous hands, I am out of position and have a hand with almost no equity or playability if called. Given my current chip lead, there is no need to force a steal with such poor cards.

I am on the button with 7-5 offsuit. While 3-handed play rewards aggression from the button, 7-5 offsuit is near the bottom of the opening range. Given that the blinds have shown some willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands, I will choose the conservative path and fold this weak hand to preserve my chip lead.

I am in the small blind and the button folded, leaving me heads-up against the big blind. Holding Ace-Eight suited is a strong position to be in, and since I am currently in the lead in terms of potential hand strength, I should raise to take control of the hand and put pressure on the big blind, rather than just limping.
Facing a raise from the small blind, my hand 6d3c is completely unplayable. It has no high card value, connectivity, or flush potential. Folding is the only reasonable decision here.

Holding 5c 7s on the button in a 3-handed game is a very weak hand with low equity. Given the current stack sizes and the tournament setting, there is no strategic justification to enter the pot with this hand. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.
95o is a very weak, unsuited hand. Being out of position in the small blind against the big blind makes it unprofitable to play. The best decision is to fold.

AJo is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game, especially from the Button. I will make a standard 3x open-raise to 300 to apply pressure on the blinds and take the initiative in the hand. This often wins the pot immediately and sets up profitable post-flop scenarios when called, given my positional advantage.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 3bb (300). King-Six offsuit is a weak hand to defend from the Small Blind, especially against a Button open where I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand. While ranges are wider in 3-handed play, this hand is not strong enough to call profitably or 3-bet as a bluff here. I choose to fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit against a button open from Pro. Given Pro's aggressive opening frequency, Ace-Nine is strong enough to play back at him. I will 3-bet to 900 to take the initiative and force him to fold or play a bloated pot where I hold a strong Ace-high hand.

While AJo is a very strong hand to open from the Button, it loses a lot of its appeal when facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind. Our opponent's 3-betting range likely consists of strong pocket pairs (TT+) and better Aces (AQ, AK) which heavily dominate us. We are in position, but calling a large 3-bet with a hand that is easily dominated often leads to tricky and unprofitable post-flop situations. Folding here is the disciplined choice, preserving our 75bb stack for better spots.

I am on the button with J7o, which is a marginal hand even in three-handed play. While it is often an opening candidate, both opponents (Flashlight and Pro) have shown willingness to 3-bet or play aggressively in recent hands. I will fold to preserve my chip lead and wait for a stronger opening hand.

I am in the small blind with a premium hand, Ace-Queen offsuit, and it has folded to me, meaning I am heads-up against the big blind. Raising is the optimal move here to take the initiative and build value with a strong range. A standard raise to 300 chips is appropriate to pressure the big blind and capitalize on my position.
I have 73o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise from the Small Blind. This hand is incredibly weak and offers no playability post-flop. Folding is the only viable option.

I am on the button with 9h Jd in a 3-handed game. This is a reasonable hand to play in position. I want to open the pot to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position. A standard raise to 300 (3 big blinds) is appropriate here to define my range and take control of the hand.
T3o is a very weak offsuit hand. Facing a raise from the Button, we have no reason to get involved out of position. The standard and optimal play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 300. In a three-handed game, A7o is a strong hand to defend the Big Blind against a Button open, as it is likely ahead of the opponent's wide opening range. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position.

I have Ace-high on a board with one overcard (King). Standard play in the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor is to check and see how they respond. Leading out here (donk betting) doesn't accomplish much against a range that includes many Kings and better hands. I will check to see the Button's action.

I missed the flop entirely on a board that favors the big blind's calling range, which includes many King, Eight, and Five combinations. With Jack-high, I have minimal showdown value and very little equity to improve. C-betting here would likely be ineffective against a solid opponent and would only inflate the pot when I am likely behind. Checking back is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and see a free turn card, where I can re-evaluate based on the next card.

After defending from the big blind with Ace-Seven offsuit, the flop came King-high. Both players checked, which often indicates that the pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) does not have a King or a strong made hand. The turn Ten of Spades completes the flush draw and brings potential straights. Since I do not hold a spade and my hand has limited showdown value, I will check again. Betting here as a bluff is risky because the board has become quite wet, and Flashlight could have checked back a marginal hand or a draw that has now improved. Checking allows me to see the river for free or evaluate a potential bet from the opponent.

I have an open-ended straight draw on this K-8-5-T board with my 9h Jd. While betting could apply pressure, checking back allows me to see the river card for free, maximizing my chances of hitting my straight while controlling the pot size. Given that my opponent has checked, checking behind keeps the pot manageable and adheres to the pot-control strategy.

The board has four spades and I hold Ace-high with no spade. Given that the hand has been checked down through the flop and turn, it is unlikely Flashlight has a very strong made hand like a King, but any single spade in their range now beats me. I have very little showdown value, but betting into a board with four spades as a bluff is risky since any spade will likely call. I will check and hope for a free showdown, though I expect to lose to any pair or any spade.

The river card, the Jack of Spades, resulted in a four-spade board and paired the Jack on the board. While I have improved to a pair of Jacks with my Jd, the board is extremely connected and any spade or any pair of Kings, Tens, Eights, or Fives would beat me. Betting here is unlikely to get value from worse hands and risks running into a flush or a better made hand. Checking behind allows me to reach showdown safely and minimize potential losses against a superior range.

On the Button with Q9 offsuit, this is a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop against calls. Given our stack size of around 73.5 BBs, there is no need to force a steal here. Folding is the standard and safest option to preserve chips for a better spot.

With 9s 7h in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I am facing the Big Blind. 97 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position. While completing the blind for 50 more is an option, it often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I am likely behind. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary chip loss.

Ace-Seven offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button in a 3-handed game. My stack is healthy, and a 3x open raise has been effective at this table. I want to put pressure on the blinds and potentially take the pot down pre-flop or play in position post-flop with an Ace-high hand.

Ace-Nine suited is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. Against a Button open, 3-betting is a superior strategic play compared to calling from the Small Blind. It allows me to seize the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, and extract value if the Button has a wide or weaker opening range. By raising to 950, I put pressure on the opener while still maintaining a stack that allows for flexibility in post-flop play.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d2h, which is one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a 3-bet from the Small Blind, my hand has virtually zero equity and playability. Folding is the only viable option.

I opened to 300 with Ace-Seven offsuit from the button, and now I am facing a 3-bet to 950 from Flashlight in the Small Blind. In a 3-handed game, A7o is a decent hand, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet from the blinds, especially when the 3-bet size is significant (over 3x). Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands (e.g., Hand #7 and Hand #21), and calling out of position or 4-betting here would likely put me in a difficult spot post-flop or risk a large portion of my stack with a mediocre hand. Since the Pro has already folded, I am only closing the action against Flashlight. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with a healthy stack. Q9o is a reasonable holding in this position to open the pot and apply pressure on the blinds. My strategy is to open-raise to 300 to steal the blinds and take control of the hand.

J6o is a very weak, unsuited hand. Facing a 3x open from the Button while in the Small Blind, calling or raising here is unprofitable due to hand strength and positional disadvantage. Folding is the only sensible choice.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (As 8h). Flashlight, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard opening size. A8o is a strong enough hand to defend against a button raise, especially in a three-handed game where hand ranges are wider. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has decent top-pair potential and high card value without bloating the pot out of position against a player who has shown aggression in previous hands.

I am first to act on a paired, relatively dry board (7s 3c 3s). My hand is currently Ace-high with an overcard and the Ace of spades, providing a backdoor flush draw. In a three-handed game, Ace-high often has significant showdown value. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. In a blind vs. blind situation, Flashlight (SB) raises to 3x. I hold A6o in the Big Blind. This is a very standard hand to defend in position against a likely wide Small Blind opening range. While a 3-bet could be considered to apply pressure, A6o plays reasonably well post-flop as a call and often has showdown value. I will call to see the flop.

I have top pair with an Ace kicker on a coordinated board with multiple draws possible (hearts and straight draws like 7-9 or 4-7). I need to continuation bet to protect my hand and force out hands with equity. A bet of 300, representing half the pot, is a standard size to define the range and build value.

I flopped middle pair with an Ace kicker on a very wet board (5h 8h 6d). Against a small blind open, my opponent's c-betting range can be quite wide, including various overcards and draws that I currently beat. Calling to keep their bluffs and weaker hands in the pot is standard. Raising would isolate me against stronger hands like top pair, overpairs, or strong combo draws. I'll call and evaluate the turn.

The board has become quite coordinated with the Ten of Hearts on the turn, completing potential straight draws and adding to the flush equity. My pair of eights is now significantly vulnerable to any heart, straight draw, or higher pair. Betting into this board would likely bloat the pot with a hand that is effectively a bluff catcher or weak value. Checking allows me to keep the pot controlled and re-evaluate my options on the river.

The turn brings a third heart and a very connected board (5, 6, 8, T). My opponent checks, which could indicate weakness or pot control. I have a marginal pair of sixes along with a weak flush draw (6h). Betting here would likely only get called by better hands, such as higher pairs, better flush draws, made flushes, or straights. Checking behind allows me to exercise pot control, realize my equity for free, and potentially catch a lucky river card.

I have improved to trip eights on the river. The board is somewhat coordinated with three hearts, but given that both the turn and the flop action indicated a lack of aggressive interest from the opponent (both checked the turn), it is highly likely that my trip eights are the best hand. I want to extract value from weaker pairs like a ten, a six, or even an eight with a worse kicker. A bet of 600 is half the pot, which should be enticing enough to get a call while protecting my hand.

The river pairs the 8 on a very connected and wet board, and the opponent bets half pot. My pair of sixes has degraded to a weak bluff catcher that loses to any Ten, Eight, overpair, and even pocket sevens or nines. The opponent's line of c-betting the flop, checking the turn, and betting the river often represents a marginal value hand like a Ten or an underpair that feels good enough to bet, or a missed draw. Given how many value hands beat me and my lack of relevant blockers to their value range, I have many better hands in my range to defend with. Folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound choice.

I am on the button with T8s, a playable hand with good post-flop potential. In a three-handed game, playing in position is a significant advantage. I will open to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, similar to my previous opening strategy.

76s is a decent suited connector, but playing it out of position against a Button raiser with the chip leader still to act in the Big Blind is risky. A call invites a squeeze from the BB, and hitting a marginal part of the board out of position can lead to losing more chips. The most solid play here is to fold and preserve the stack.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Deuce offsuit (Ah 2d). The Button (Flashlight) has opened for a 3x raise to 300. With the Small Blind folding, I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 2.25:1 odds. A2o is a standard defending hand in the Big Blind against a Button open, especially given my significant stack lead and the pot odds provided. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a paired board (8-8-T). My Ace-high has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out with. I will check to the Button (Flashlight) and see how they act. This allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a cheap turn card if they check back.

I have flopped a monster hand with a full house (Tens full of Eights) on a board of 8d-8h-Th. My opponent checked to me, and I need to start extracting value immediately to build a significant pot. A bet of 350 into the 650 pot is a balanced size that will likely get called by any pair, flush draw, or straight draw the opponent might be holding.

On a board of 8-8-T, my Ace-high (A2o) has very little equity and no direct draws. Flashlight's continuation bet of 350 into a 650 pot is a standard play from the pre-flop aggressor. While I might occasionally have the best hand if he is air-bluffing, I am out of position and have almost no way to improve my hand significantly on the turn. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

7d 8d is a strong suited connector and an excellent hand to open from the Button in a 3-handed game. Raising to my standard 3x (300) size applies pressure on the blinds to steal the pot directly, and if called, I will have a very playable hand with positional advantage post-flop.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. While King-high has some strength in three-handed play, K2o is very weak and plays poorly out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and better spots will arise, I will fold this hand instead of calling or 3-betting.

My hand, Ten-Four offsuit, is too weak to defend profitably against an open raise from the button, even with the offered pot odds. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I have more equity or position.

I am on the button with Six-Deuce offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the active play from both the Small Blind (Flashlight) and the Big Blind (Pro) in previous hands, attempting a steal with such a low-equity hand is not advisable. Preserving my stack and waiting for better spots is the most professional play here.

Holding 3s 8d in the small blind is a very weak hand. Given that there's no pre-flop raise from the button, I could potentially complete the small blind, but 3-8 offsuit has poor playability and equity. Folding is the most disciplined play to conserve my stack and avoid playing marginal hands out of position.

I am in the button position in a three-handed game, which allows for a wider opening range. While ten-three suited is a marginal hand, it has some potential for stealing the blinds given the aggressive nature of the game and my position. Raising to 300 is a standard move to test the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop.
Holding 9c 2s, an extremely weak starting hand, in the small blind facing a raise from the button. This is an easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack of Clubs and Seven of Diamonds. Flashlight has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. The pot odds are 200 to call into a 450 pot (requiring ~31% equity). While J7o has decent raw equity against a wide Button opening range, it is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position post-flop. Given my current chip lead and the 3x raise size, there is no need to defend with a marginal hand that often leads to difficult spots on the flop. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

With 97o on the button, my hand is too weak to justify a standard open raise, especially with both opponents having larger stacks and showing a willingness to play back. I'll fold and look for a better spot to commit my chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 6c 7h. The Button has folded, leaving just me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). While 6-7 offsuit is a weak hand, in a three-handed game and specifically in a blind-versus-blind battle, it has some potential. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call the remaining 50 chips. I will call to see a flop, intending to play cautiously if I don't improve significantly, especially given Flashlight's history of aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with a weak hand, Jack-three offsuit. Since the Small Blind limped in, I have the option to check and see the flop for free. Raising here would bloat the pot with a subpar hand, so checking is the most strategic and disciplined play.

On this King-Queen-Four all-heart flop, I have absolutely nothing: no pair, no draw, and only the 7 of hearts as a very weak backdoor flush component. Given that I'm out of position against Flashlight and this board heavily hits a typical Big Blind's range (which includes many high cards and hearts), I will check. If Flashlight bets, I will likely fold as there is very little potential to continue.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-three, holding one heart on a board showing three hearts. Since I have no pair and no strong draw, betting into the pot would be a bluff without sufficient equity. Checking is the most prudent and disciplined action to keep the pot small and see the turn for free.

On the turn, the board pairs the low card (4c). I still have absolutely no hand value and no draw with 6c 7h. Flashlight checked back on the flop, which might indicate weakness, but betting into this board with total air is risky given the high-card and flush possibilities. I will check again, intending to fold if Flashlight bets, as I have zero equity in this pot.

The board has paired with a four, and the presence of three hearts makes it a dangerous texture. My Jack-high hand has almost no showdown value and minimal equity. Betting here would essentially be a speculative bluff with little chance of success against a player who has checked twice. Checking is the most prudent path to reach the river with minimal risk.

The board has double-paired with Kings and Fours, and a Queen as the high kicker (K-K-4-4-Q). My hole cards (6c 7h) do not improve this hand. Currently, I am 'playing the board,' meaning my best five-card hand is the board itself. Flashlight also plays the board unless they have an Ace (which would make K-K-A-4-4), or a King, Queen, or Four (which would give them a full house). Since Flashlight checked on the flop and turn, it's possible they have a weak hand, but even an Ace-high would beat me here. Betting to try to force a fold on a chopping hand is unnecessary in such a small pot (200 chips). I will check and hope for a check-back to chop the pot or see if my 7-high somehow holds if they have even lower hole cards, though an Ace is very much in their range.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-high (Jc 3h) on a board of 4h Qh Kh 4c Kd. My hand has no showdown value, and there is no reason to attempt a bluff here given that both opponents have checked down the entire street. Checking is the standard play to reach showdown with a worthless hand.

In a 3-handed game, Ten-Nine offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I'll raise to 300, which has been the standard opening size throughout the tournament, to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds.

With T7o in the small blind, this is a clear fold. I am out of position against the button, and the hand does not play well post-flop. There is no reason to risk chips with such a weak holding, especially given my stack size is sufficient to wait for better opportunities.
9c 7s offsuit is a weak holding, especially out of position against a button raise from the chip leader. There's no reason to defend the big blind with this hand; the optimal move is to fold.
Pocket sixes is a strong pocket pair in a three-handed game. Opening with a standard raise is the optimal way to take control of the hand, build the pot, and capitalize on my position as the button.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (93o). The Button has raised to 300. This is an easy fold as playing this hand out of position against a raise is not profitable.

I am in the Big Blind with 3s 7d. The button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, which is a standard open. I am on the button with 93 offsuit, which is an extremely weak starting hand. With two players left to act behind me, the correct move is to simply fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J3o. Pro has already folded from the Button. The pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call or 200 to raise. J3o is a weak hand even in a blind-vs-blind situation, and Flashlight in the Big Blind has been reasonably aggressive. Completing the small blind is an option, but given the hand's poor playability post-flop, folding is a standard and safe play to preserve my chip lead.

I am on the button with 9-7 offsuit. This is a mediocre hand, but in a 3-handed game, it's often a reasonable hand to open or call from the button. The pot is 150 and it's 100 to call if I were to just complete, but I am the dealer (Button) and the action is on me first. Actually, looking at the log, the Small Blind and Big Blind have already posted their blinds (50/100). As the Button, I have the option to fold, call, or raise. 97o is a marginal opening hand. Given the stack sizes and the need to maintain a healthy lead, I'll fold this hand to wait for a better spot, as 97o doesn't play very well post-flop if I face aggression from Flashlight or Pro.

The hand 9-2 offsuit is among the weakest possible starting hands. Playing from the small blind against the big blind with such a poor holding is not profitable. Folding is the correct strategic decision to preserve my stack and avoid playing in a marginal spot out of position.

I am in the button position with 9h 5h. This is a speculative hand with suited connectors. While marginal, opening from the button is a standard aggressive strategy in a short-handed game to steal the blinds. Raising to 300 provides enough pressure to potentially take down the pot pre-flop.

My hand is 8h 4s, which is an extremely weak, unconnected, and offsuit hand. The Button (Flashlight) has opened with a 3x raise to 300. Being out of position in the Small Blind with one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em, the only logical action is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened to 300. With the Small Blind folding, I need to call 200 more into a total pot of 450. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-inflating the pot out of position with a hand that has poor post-flop playability if the Ace doesn't land.

On this 6d-9s-Js flop, I have missed completely with my Ace-Deuce offsuit, though I do hold the Ace of Spades for a backdoor nut flush draw. If they check back, I get a free card. If they bet, I will evaluate based on their sizing, but generally, Ace-high is weak on this coordinated board.

I hold middle pair (9s) with a weak kicker on a somewhat coordinated board. While I have position, betting into the Big Blind with such a weak hand could lead to getting check-raised or over-committed in a pot where I am likely ahead but vulnerable to many turns. Checking behind is the best way to control the pot size and realize my equity.

The turn 6c pairs the board. Flashlight checked back on a relatively wet flop (Js-9s-6d), which suggests they likely do not have a strong Jack or a high-equity draw, as those hands typically bet for protection or value on that board. My Ace-high (As2c) still has some showdown value against their air and overcard hands (like KQ or QT). Checking is the most prudent play here to keep the pot small and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or catch an Ace on the river. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the standard move.

I have a pair of 9s on a 6-9-J-6 board. With the board paired, my hand is vulnerable to any higher pair or a better 6. Since my opponent checked the turn after checking the flop, they likely have a marginal hand or are playing trappy. Betting here risks bloating the pot with a weak holding against a potential trap or a better hand. I have Ace-high (As2c) on a board of 6d-9s-Js-6c-7h. The pre-flop aggressor (Flashlight) has checked back both the flop and the turn. While Ace-high has some showdown value against missed overcards (like KQ or QT), it's unlikely to be the best hand if the action gets heavy. By checking, I am looking to realize my showdown value for free. If Flashlight bets, I will have to evaluate their sizing, but given the passive line they've taken, a check is the most prudent way to reach a cheap showdown.

The board is 6d-9s-Js-6c-7h. My hand is a pair of 9s. The opponent has checked the flop, turn, and river, which suggests they either have a marginal hand, a missed draw, or are potentially trapping with a strong hand like a 6 or a Jack. Given my pair of 9s, I have decent showdown value but little potential to get called by worse hands if I bet. Betting would only isolate me against hands that beat me or force me to fold if the opponent check-raises. Checking behind is the most disciplined way to reach showdown and realize my equity.

I am on the button with 5-8 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. With a stack size around 64 big blinds and aggressive opponents in the blinds, attempting to steal here is unprofitable. Folding is the standard and optimal play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak hand. While it only costs 50 more to call, playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is generally unprofitable in the long run. Since the Button has already folded, I have no interest in defending or bluffing with this specific holding. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am on the button with 9h 6c. This is a weak hand even in a three-handed game. While I have a significant chip lead, opening such a wide range can lead to difficult spots post-flop against Flashlight or Pro. I'll stick to a more disciplined range and fold this hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 2c 6d, which is an extremely weak starting hand. With no raise from the Button, I could complete the bet to see a flop cheaply, but playing this hand out of position against the Big Blind is generally a losing strategy. It is best to fold, preserve my stack, and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit. This is a marginal hand that lacks sufficient strength to play profitably against the blinds. While I am in position, it is best to conserve my stack and wait for a stronger starting hand rather than contesting a pot with a weak holding.

With King-Ten offsuit in the small blind and the action folded to me, I have a strong enough hand to raise for value and to attempt to steal the blind. Raising to 3x (300) takes the initiative and puts pressure on the big blind.

I am in the big blind holding Ad2s. Pro has raised from the small blind to 300. Given the pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 400) and the fact that this is a blind-vs-blind situation where Pro's range is likely wide, A2o is a strong enough hand to defend with. I will call to see the flop and use my position post-flop.

As the pre-flop aggressor, the Ace-high rainbow flop heavily favors my perceived range. Although I missed the flop completely with King-Ten high, making a small continuation bet of 1/3 pot (200 chips) is a cheap and effective way to represent the Ace. This bet should successfully fold out my opponent's missed broadways and lower pocket pairs, and if I get called, I can easily give up on later streets.

I have top pair with an Ace on a dry board (As-9h-4d). While my kicker is very weak, top pair is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. Pro's bet of 200 into a 600 pot (now 800) is a standard continuation bet size. Raising at this stage might force out bluffs and only get called by better Aces or hands that have me beat. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot and see how the turn develops.

My plan on the flop was to make a continuation bet and give up if called, as an Ace-high board hits the pre-flop caller's range decently. The 3 of spades on the turn doesn't improve my King-high, nor does it present a scary card to barrel as a bluff. My opponent's flop call indicates they likely have an Ace, a 9, or a pocket pair, which they are unlikely to fold to a turn bet. Checking and giving up on the hand is the safest line here to preserve my stack.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (Aces, 2 kicker) on a relatively dry board. Pro checked the turn after betting the flop. While I likely have the best hand in this blind-vs-blind scenario, my kicker makes my hand vulnerable to better Aces. Checking back provides pot control, disguises the strength of my hand, and allows me to see the river for free. If Pro is checking a medium-strength hand or a draw, I can still get value on the river or induce a bluff.

The second Ace on the river makes it difficult to convincingly represent an Ace, as the combinations are heavily reduced and my turn check looks like a give-up or pot control. King-high has some marginal showdown value against missed broadway floats (like QJ or JT), and I would fold to a river bet. Checking is the most conservative and optimal line here, avoiding risking more chips on a bluff that is unlikely to fold out opponent's marginal made hands, such as a 9 or a small pocket pair.

I have Three of a Kind (Aces) with a 9 and 4 kicker from the board. The board paired the Ace on the river (As-9h-4d-3s-Ah). Pro raised from the small blind and continuation-bet the flop, then checked both the turn and the river. This line often indicates a medium-strength hand like a 9, a middle pocket pair (55-88), or a total bluff that has given up. While my kicker is weak (I lose to any Ace higher than A4), it is highly likely Pro would have bet the river for value if he held any Ace himself. By betting, I can extract value from a 9 or potentially a middle pair that might call a reasonably sized bet. A bet of 500 into the 1000 pot is a standard value-bet size that can get called by hands I beat while not risking too much if Pro was trappy with a superior Ace.

My King-high is simply too weak to call a half-pot bet on the river. As I planned on the turn, my strategy was to give up the hand once my flop continuation bet was called. The opponent's river bet could be for thin value with a pair of 9s or a small pocket pair, a slowplayed Ace, or a bluff with missed broadways. Even if they are bluffing, my King-Ten high is at the bottom of my range and is a very weak bluff catcher. Folding is the most disciplined and optimal decision to preserve my stack.

Ace-King suited is a premium starting hand. From the Button, raising to 3x the big blind (300 chips) is the standard play to build the pot and thin the field, ensuring I play a heads-up or take down the blinds immediately.

I have a small pocket pair (22) in the small blind. The button (Pro) has raised to 300, which is a standard opening range. While 22 is a weak pair, it has good value for 'set mining' if I can see a flop cheaply. Calling 250 more to potentially win Pro's stack (roughly 5,400 behind) provides sufficient implied odds (over 20:1). I will play fit-or-fold on the flop, looking for a Deuce to improve my hand significantly.

7c 3d is an extremely weak hand with poor playability, especially when facing a raise and a call. Even though I am getting favorable pot odds to call, the likelihood of hitting a flop that allows me to continue is very low. Folding is the disciplined play to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

On a highly coordinated board of Js 7d 8s, my pocket deuces (2h 2s) have very little showdown value and are vulnerable to almost any overcard. Since I am out of position and missed my set, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor. If Pro bets, I plan to fold as this board connects well with a standard button opening range (Jacks, connectors like 98, 78, or straight/flush draws) and my hand has almost no potential to improve.

The Js 7d 8s flop is very wet and connects heavily with the Small Blind's pre-flop calling range, which contains many medium pairs, suited connectors, and broadways. I have missed the flop completely, holding only Ace-King high and a backdoor diamond draw. Betting here as a continuation bet is likely to get called or raised by many hands, so checking behind to realize my equity and see a free turn card is the safest and most optimal play.

The turn Ten of Diamonds creates a very coordinated board where any Nine makes a straight. My pocket deuces have no showdown value and no realistic chance of improving to a winning hand. I will check and fold to any bet from my opponent.

On the turn, I pick up a massive draw: the nut flush draw with the Ad Kd, plus a gutshot to the Broadway straight with any Queen. Flash has checked to me twice, showing weakness. Betting here serves as a strong semi-bluff; it puts pressure on marginal pairs and weaker draws, allowing me to win the pot uncontested, while building the pot for the times I hit my premium outs on the river. A bet of around half to two-thirds pot is ideal.

The board is extremely coordinated (Js-7d-8s-Td), with any Nine making a straight and various flush/straight draws present. My pocket deuces have no showdown value and no realistic way to improve to the best hand. Pro checked the flop but is now betting into this dangerous turn card. Given I am out of position and have no equity, folding is the only disciplined move.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven of Diamonds, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I have a significant chip lead and want to put pressure on the blinds. A standard open-raise to 300 is appropriate here to take control of the pot early.

I hold pocket Jacks, a very strong premium hand. Flash is the button and has been aggressive, so I want to take control of the pot and avoid letting the big blind see a cheap flop, while simultaneously building value against a likely wide opening range from the button. A 3-bet to 1000 is an appropriate sizing to isolate and define the strength of our hands early.
I hold K9o in the big blind facing an open raise from the button and a large 3-bet from the small blind. This hand is far too weak to call or 4-bet, so folding is the only correct decision.

I have Ace-Seven of Diamonds on the button and opened for 300. Flashlight in the small blind has 3-bet to 1000. In a 3-handed game, ranges are wider, and A7s is a very strong hand to play in position. While Flashlight has shown aggression in previous hands, folding here would be too tight. A 4-bet is an option but might polarize the pot too early against a range that could contain better Aces or pocket pairs. Calling allows me to utilize my positional advantage and see a flop with a hand that has both high-card value and nut-flush potential.

The flop is extremely dry and paired (2s 2d Qs), which reduces the likelihood that either player has connected significantly unless they hold a Queen. My pocket Jacks are currently an overpair to the deuces but are behind any Queen. Given I am out of position as the pre-flop aggressor, checking is a standard play to control the pot size and induce bluffs from the button, or to realize equity cheaply if the board remains static. Betting here may only get called by hands that have me beat or fold out the hands I dominate, so checking is the most strategic option.

On a board of 2s-2d-Qs after a 3-bet pot pre-flop, Flashlight's check is somewhat ambiguous. They could be trapping with a Queen or a high pocket pair, or they could have missed with hands like AK or AJ. Since I have Ace-high with a backdoor diamond draw and showdown value, checking back is a prudent move. It allows me to control the size of the pot in position and see how the turn develops without overcommitting against a potentially stronger range. Betting here likely only gets called by better hands and folds out hands I am currently beating.

I have flopped a Full House (Jacks full of Deuces) on a board of 2s 2d Qs Jh, which is an extremely strong hand. I am currently holding the effective nuts, with the only hands beating me being pocket Queens (QQ) or pocket Deuces (22). Given the check-check on the flop, there is a good chance my opponent is holding a range of hands (possibly including an Ace, King, or Queen) that they may be willing to call with. I need to start building the pot to extract value from their weaker holdings or missed draws. Betting 1200 into a 2100 pot represents a strong, value-oriented sizing that sets up a river shove if called, while still appearing like a continuation bet or a thin value bet.

Flashlight's 1200 bet into a 2100 pot on the turn after checking the flop is a classic 'delayed continuation bet' or an attempt to take down the pot after both of us checked the flop. My Ace-high (Ad7d) still has some showdown value and the board is somewhat coordinated (2s-2d-Qs-Jh). However, I am facing a significant bet (over 1/2 pot) in a 3-bet pot. Flashlight's range for 3-betting pre-flop includes many hands that now beat me, like KQ, AQ, or even JJ/TT that they might have checked on the flop. While I have the nut flush draw potential if a diamond hits, there is only one diamond on the board (2d). My Ace-high is likely behind, and calling here with only one street left to improve is risky against a player who has shown aggression. However, given the pot odds (3300:1200) and my position, a fold is probably the safest play to preserve my chip lead, as I am likely drawing thin or facing a better Ace. But wait, looking at the previous street, I have Ad7d and the board has 2d. I don't have a flush draw. I only have Ace high. Calling 1200 to see a river with Ace high in a 3-bet pot is a losing play long term against this betting pattern.

I am in the button position with King-Deuce offsuit (Kd 3h). This is a very weak hand in a three-handed game, offering poor playability and dominated by any Ace or King with a better kicker. Even with the button advantage, playing this hand is negative expected value. I will fold.

J2o is a very weak hand. Being in the small blind, even though the button folded, attempting to steal or complete with such a marginal holding is generally unprofitable. I will be out of position post-flop if the big blind calls, and the hand has virtually no playability. Folding is the standard and safest play.
Holding 73 suited on the Button is far too weak of a hand to open or play, despite having position. The best and most standard play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 5c. It has been folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind to contend with. While I could attempt a steal or complete the bet, 65 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Flashlight (Big Blind) has shown aggression in several recent hands, and I don't want to commit chips with a sub-optimal hand when I currently hold the chip lead.
