I have King-Queen offsuit on the Button. This is a strong starting hand, especially from late position. With 33 big blinds, a standard raise to 300 is optimal to steal the blinds or build a pot in position if I get called.

I am in the Small Blind with 6h 2c, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is the short stack with only 3,000 chips remaining. While I have a massive chip lead, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand out of position against a player who is likely looking to commit their stack soon. Folding is the most standard and disciplined play here.

I hold Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in this position. Pro is on the button and has opened the pot with a relatively short stack. A 3-bet will isolate the Pro, put significant pressure on their 3000-chip stack, and allow me to take control of the hand. 900 is a standard, effective 3-bet size given the current raise to 300.

I opened KQo on the Button, which is a standard steal/value raise. However, Flashlight 3-bets to 9 BB from the Big Blind. KQ offsuit performs poorly against a standard 3-betting range, as it is heavily dominated by hands like AK, AQ, and big pocket pairs. Calling would leave me with an awkward stack-to-pot ratio post-flop, and shoving 33 BB is an overplay against a BB 3-bet. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play here.

I am on the button with KJo, which is a very strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. I have a significant chip lead over both opponents, especially 'Pro' who is under 30 big blinds. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the blinds, as this hand performs well against most of their defending ranges.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong hand in a three-handed game, especially from the small blind. Flash has opened from the button, which is a wide range. I want to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or set up a continuation bet on the flop. A raise to 900 is a standard 3-bet size to put pressure on the opener while still being manageable if they 4-bet.

Facing a Button open and a Small Blind 3-bet, KQ offsuit is too weak to call, and a 4-bet shove is overly risky against their typical 3-betting ranges. Folding is the standard play to preserve my 29 BB stack.

KJo is a strong hand in 3-handed play, especially when opening from the button. Flashlight's 3-bet from the small blind is a standard move, but since I have a significant chip lead and will be in position for the rest of the hand, I have the necessary odds and leverage to see a flop. KJo performs reasonably well against a wide 3-betting range in a short-handed tournament. I will call the additional 600 to see the flop in position.

The flop is monotone, which is a dangerous texture. However, I am the aggressor having 3-bet pre-flop. I hold the Jack of Spades, giving me a secondary flush draw, which provides some equity. Checking might invite a bet from Flash that I cannot easily call. A continuation bet of roughly one-third of the pot (600 into 1900) is standard here to maintain initiative and potentially fold out hands like small pairs or high-card hands that didn't connect with the spades, while also testing where Flash stands.

The flop is extremely dangerous as it is monotone with three spades (3s, 4s, Qs). I have King-high with no spades, meaning I have no flush draw and no made hand. Flashlight was the pre-flop 3-bettor, which indicates a strong range that often includes high pairs or strong Ace-X hands, many of which may contain a spade. Given that I have zero equity on this board and no way to improve to a winning hand reliably if the opponent has even a small piece or a draw, I should not commit any more chips. Fold is the most disciplined and correct play here.

I am on the button with 6c3c. While these suited connectors are weak, I have position for the rest of the hand. The cost to enter is only 100 chips to potentially play a 250 chip pot (plus whatever the blinds do). Given the stack depths, I'll limp in to see a flop, hoping to hit a flush or straight draw, and play cautiously if I miss.

With a short stack of 28.5 BBs, playing a speculative suited one-gapper like 64s out of position against a Button limp is unnecessarily risky. The Big Blind has a massive stack and could easily squeeze, forcing me to fold and waste chips. Preserving my stack for better reshove or steal opportunities is the priority here, so folding is the optimal play.

I have Ace-high (Ac 3d) in the Big Blind. Flashlight limped from the Button, and the Small Blind folded. Ace-high is likely ahead of a typical button limping range. By raising to 400, I am putting pressure on the limper and attempting to take down the pot pre-flop or define the hand before seeing the flop out of position.

After my initial limp, the Big Blind raised to 400. 6c3c is a very weak hand to defend with, even in position. The pot odds do not justify calling with such a marginal holding against a raise. Folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

94 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand with virtually no playability post-flop. From the button, raising or calling with this hand is long-term negative expected value (EV). Folding is the only optimal decision here to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit (Ts 2d), which is a very weak starting hand. The Button (Pro) has already folded. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, T2o performs poorly post-flop and I would be playing out of position against Flashlight in the Big Blind. Given my chip lead and the poor quality of my hand, there is no need to take unnecessary risks or try to bluff with such a weak holding.

I am on the button with Jack-Seven offsuit. While not a powerhouse hand, it is a standard opening candidate in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds, especially since the Big Blind (Pro) has a very short stack of 27.5 big blinds. Raising allows me to take control of the pot early or potentially take the blinds immediately.

King-Treys offsuit is a very weak hand, especially out of position in the Small Blind against an aggressive Button open. The risk of playing a potentially bloated pot with marginal strength is not worth the investment. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am in the Big Blind with 92 offsuit, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button, I have no equity to justify a call or a 3-bet. Folding is the only sensible and profitable play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Three suited, which is a strong hand in a 3-handed game. I have position and the initiative. Raising to 300 is a standard open to potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position.

Q6o is a weak hand, especially out of position from the small blind against a button raise. With a stack of 27 big blinds, I need to preserve chips and wait for a better spot to commit chips. Folding is the optimal play here.

In the Big Blind, I hold Qs9s, which is a strong suited connector. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) has folded. The pot currently has 450 chips, and it costs me 200 more to call. I am getting 2.25:1 odds on a call. Qs9s plays well post-flop, especially in a heads-up pot against a Button opening range which is typically wide. Defending the Big Blind here is standard. I'll call and evaluate the flop.

I have missed the flop with Queen high. This board (7h 2c 6h) is relatively low and doesn't connect well with my Qs9s. Standard procedure is to check to the pre-flop raiser. I'll check and see if they continuation bet, evaluating whether my overcards are worth a call based on their bet size and the board texture.

The flop is dry and low, which generally misses a raising range but can hit a calling range. However, I have the nut flush draw with my Ace of Clubs and the betting initiative. A continuation bet here is standard to test the strength of my opponent and potentially pick up the pot immediately. If called, I have outs to improve, and if check-raised, I can evaluate my position with Ace-high.

On a flop of 7h-2c-6h, I have two overcards (Queen and Nine) and a backdoor spade draw. Flashlight's bet of 300 into a 650 pot is roughly 46% of the pot, giving me odds of about 3:1 (I need to be right or improve about 24% of the time). Given that Flashlight is on the Button and likely has a wide opening range, this c-bet is standard for them regardless of whether they connected with the board. I have enough equity with my overcards and the backdoor potential to justify a call, especially since I'm closing the action on this street. I will call and see if the turn improves my hand or provides more information.

The turn pairs the board with the 7c. I still have no pair and am holding Queen high. There is no reason to bet into the pre-flop aggressor here. I will check and see if Flashlight checks behind, which might allow me to realize some showdown value or see a free river. If they bet, I'll most likely fold as my equity is low.

The board is now paired with the 7c, and I have a club flush draw with my Ace of Clubs and Trey of Clubs (four clubs total with the 2c, 7c, Ac, 3c). My opponent checked to me on the turn after calling the flop. Since I have position and a draw, checking behind allows me to realize my equity for free and potentially hit the nut flush on the river while keeping the pot size controlled.

The river brings an Ace of Spades (As), which is a significant scare card. On the turn (7c), my opponent (Flashlight) checked behind after I checked. This check usually suggests a capped range—either a marginal made hand (like a 6, a 2, or a small pocket pair) that is looking for a cheap showdown, or a total air hand that has given up. My Queen-high has almost no showdown value against his range. However, the Ace is a card that connects well with my Big Blind defending range. By betting now, I can represent that I have hit the Ace or was perhaps slow-playing a 7 on the turn. A bet of roughly half the pot (600) is designed to fold out his marginal one-pair hands and missed draws that might otherwise beat me at showdown.

My opponent checked the flop and turn, then led out for 600 on the river after the Ace of Spades hit. This line is somewhat polarized, as it could be a missed flush draw turning into a bluff, or a value bet from a hand like a 7 (trips). However, given my top pair (Aces), I have enough showdown value to call. Folding would be too weak here, and raising for value is unlikely to get called by worse hands, while potentially walking into a stronger hand like a full house or trips. Calling is the most balanced and profitable approach.

Q9 suited is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game, especially when acting first from the Button. With a 27 BB stack, I have enough room to open with a standard 3x raise to either steal the blinds or play a pot in position.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Six offsuit. The Button (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is relatively short-stacked with only 2400 chips (24 BBs). Ace-Six is a decent hand in a three-handed game, and I am getting good odds to see a flop. While 3-betting to isolate is an option, a call allows me to evaluate the flop without potentially committing myself to a pre-flop shove from Pro's short stack. I will call the 250 more and see how the board develops.

T5o is a very weak hand to defend with, even with the discounted price of 200 into a 700 pot. With two opponents, including an initial raiser, I am highly likely to be behind and facing a difficult situation on the flop. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the prudent decision.

The flop is Q-J-5 rainbow. I have Ace-high with no direct draw. The Button (Pro) was the pre-flop raiser and has a very short stack of 2400. In a three-handed game, this board hits a lot of a Button's raising range (KJ, QJ, K10, etc.), though I do have an overcard. I will check to the aggressor. If he bets, I will have to decide based on his sizing, but with two overcards to my Ace on the board, my hand is currently quite weak.

I flopped top pair with a decent kicker and a backdoor flush draw on a moderately connected board (5h Jd Qs). Checking behind gives free cards to various straight draws and overcards. A continuation bet of about half-pot extracts value from hands like Jx, worse Qx, and straight draws (like KT or T9), while also protecting my equity and keeping the pot size manageable relative to my stack.

On this Q-J-5 rainbow board, I have Ace-high with very little connectivity. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 300 bet into a 700 pot. This board texture heavily favors a standard opening range from the button, which includes many Queens, Jacks, and straight draws like K-10 or K-9. With only 2,100 chips remaining in Pro's stack, my Ace-high is in a difficult spot; I am likely behind, and even if I am ahead of a bluff, I have poor visibility on future streets. Calling here would put me in a position where I might have to call off against a shove on the turn with only a marginal holding. Therefore, folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving play.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. My goal is to apply pressure and pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. Given the stack sizes, specifically Pro's short stack in the Big Blind (3000), I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300. This maintains my table image and allows me to see how the blinds react.

A6o is a weak hand to defend from the small blind against a button open, especially being out of position for the rest of the hand. It is better to fold and wait for a better spot to preserve the stack.

With King-Eight offsuit in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Button, we have a marginal at best hand. Being the short stack with 30 BBs, calling out of position with a hand that has strong reverse implied odds is generally a leak. Folding to preserve our stack for a better spot is the optimal play.

I am in the button position with Jack-Eight offsuit, which is a weak starting hand. In a three-handed game, this hand lacks the strength to profitably open against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and prudent choice to preserve my stack for a more favorable situation.

With 8h 9s offsuit in the Small Blind and a relatively short stack of 29.5 BBs, playing a marginal, speculative hand out of position against the massive chip leader in the Big Blind is unprofitable. Folding to preserve the stack for better starting hands and positional advantages is the safest and most standard play.

Holding 4d2s on the Button is an extremely weak hand with practically no playability. Given my short stack relative to the opponents, there's no justification to play this pre-flop. The clear standard action is to fold and wait for better starting hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. The Button has already folded, leaving only the Big Blind to beat. While I have a significant chip lead, 5-2 offsuit has very little post-flop playability even in a heads-up pot. Folding here is the most disciplined play to avoid unnecessary losses with a trash hand.

I am on the button with a suited connector (4c 6c). With my large stack and position, I want to apply pressure on the blinds. Pro is the short stack and might be looking for a spot to shove, but a standard 3x raise is the most effective way to either take down the blinds immediately or see a flop in position. If either blind defends, I have post-flop flexibility. If Pro shoves, I can reassess based on the odds, though I'd likely fold this specific hand.

I am in the small blind with King-Eight suited. The button has opened for a standard raise. Although I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand, the pot odds are favorable to call, and this hand has enough equity to see a flop against a button open.

Holding 6-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Facing a raise from the Button and a call from the Small Blind, folding is the only sensible choice to preserve my stack.

I am out of position against the button, Flash. The board is dry, and my K-high is currently behind any pair, but I have overcards and backdoor equity. Checking is the standard play here to evaluate the opponent's continuation bet or see a turn card cheaply.

I have hit bottom pair (fours) on a relatively dry board of 2s-Td-4d. After raising from the button pre-flop, I want to maintain my aggression with a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (roughly 43% of the pot) is large enough to fold out hands that missed the flop entirely (like overcards) while being a cheap enough price to pay to see if Flashlight has a Ten or a diamond draw. If I am called, I can evaluate the turn and potentially check back to get to a cheap showdown.

The board is dry, and my King-high does not have enough equity to justify a call against a bet of nearly half the pot, especially being out of position. Without a pair or a meaningful draw, it is best to fold and preserve my stack for a more favorable spot.

I am on the button with an Ace-five offsuit. This is a strong enough hand to open from this position to attempt to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I will raise to 300, which has been a standard opening size throughout the tournament.
I am in the Small Blind with an extremely weak offsuit hand (3s 7d) facing a raise from the Button. This is a very straightforward fold.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300. The Small Blind (Pro) has folded. This leaves me to decide whether to call 200 more into a pot of 450. K4o is a marginal hand, but getting better than 2:1 odds and being at the end of the action, I can justify a call to see a flop, especially since Flashlight has been quite active. Given the stack sizes, I don't want to bloat the pot out of position against the most aggressive stack remaining, but folding for only 200 more feels too tight in a three-handed game.

I have King-high on a connected board (8-J-7) that likely hits the button's opening range better than mine. I have no pair and no real draw. I am out of position against Flashlight, who has been aggressive. The best play here is to check and see how the button reacts. I am prepared to fold to most standard-sized bets since I have virtually no equity on this texture.

I am on the button with Ace-five high on a Jack-high board. The flop has missed my hand completely, providing no pair and no significant draw. As the pre-flop aggressor who has been checked to, I have the option to continuation bet, but betting into a board that hits the Big Blind's calling range frequently is likely to result in a call or a check-raise, committing me to a pot I have very little equity in. Checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot size, take a free card, and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board is 8d-Jh-7c-Qc. I have King-high with no draws. The flop went check-check after I checked to the pre-flop raiser (Flashlight). The Queen is a significant card that connects well with both our ranges, but since I have no equity and no real way to represent a hand that would successfully bluff the button off a pair on this coordinated board, checking is the only viable option. I will fold to any bet.

I am on the button with Ace-five high on a Jack-high board. The flop was checked through, and my opponent has checked the turn again. I have no pair and no draw, so my hand has very low showdown value. Betting would effectively be a bluff into a board that connects well with the big blind's defending range (8, J, 7, Q are all cards they defend). Checking back allows me to reach a cheap showdown or potentially take a free card to the river, minimizing losses with a weak hand.

On the river, the board is 8-J-7-Q-T. I have King-high with no pair or straight. The board is very coordinated, and while Flashlight has checked twice, my hand has almost no showdown value as I lose to any pair and all Ace-high hands. Since the pot is small and I am out of position, I will check. If Flashlight bets, I intend to fold as it's unlikely they are bluffing into this specific board texture after checking twice, and I have no equity to call.

I have reached the river with Ace-high on a board of 8d-Jh-7c-Qc-Td. This board is extremely coordinated and connects heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, as any 9 or King now makes a straight, and many other cards pair the board. With no pair, no draw, and only Ace-high, I have effectively zero showdown value against any pair. Checking back is the only sensible play; betting would be a thin bluff into a board that is likely to have hit the opponent's range, and there is no reason to inflate a pot where I am almost certainly behind.

I am on the button with 7s 5d offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, and with only 2800 chips left, I need to preserve my stack for a better spot to try and double up or play profitably. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 92o, which is a very weak hand. The Button has already folded. While I have a significant chip lead, 92o is not a hand I want to play out of position against Flashlight, who has a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary risk with bottom-tier cards.

I am on the button with Jack-Five offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. While I am the chip leader and in the best position, J5o does not perform well against the defending ranges of the blinds, especially since Pro is short-stacked (27 BB) and likely to look for a double-up opportunity by shoving over a button raise. I will fold and wait for a more favorable hand to apply pressure.

I am in the small blind with T5s after the button folded. Completing the small blind (calling the remaining 50) is a standard play to see a flop cheaply with a suited hand. This minimizes risk against the big blind while allowing me to realize my equity.
I have a very weak offsuit hand (84o) in the big blind. The small blind limped, so I can check to see a free flop. There is no reason to invest more chips with this holding.

The flop is 6d 2h Ts and I hold Td 5d. I have middle pair, but my kicker is weak and I am out of position. In a limped pot, checking is the standard play here to keep the pot small and see the turn for free, as betting into the big blind with a marginal hand risks bloating the pot unnecessarily when I am likely ahead but vulnerable.

With 8-high and no draw on this relatively dry flop, I have no equity to bet for value and it's not a high-percentage bluffing spot. Checking behind takes a free card and controls the pot size given my short stack.

I am in the small blind with middle pair (Tens) on a board of 6d 2h Ts 7s. The pot is very small (200), and I checked the flop, indicating a desire to keep the pot controlled. The turn card 7s opens up straight draw possibilities (such as 8-9), but my pair remains a bluff catcher rather than a value hand. Betting here would likely only be called by better hands or cause a fold from worse hands that might otherwise bluff later. Checking again is the most cautious and strategic approach to realize my equity and reach a cheap showdown.

Flashlight has shown weakness by checking both the flop and the turn after just limping pre-flop. With an 8-high and a gutshot straight draw, I have very little showdown value but enough equity to make a semi-bluff. Stabbing at the pot here with a half-pot bet should often win the pot immediately against a weak range.

The turn bet from the Big Blind is small, only 100 chips into a 200-chip pot. My hand, middle pair (Tens), has decent showdown value. The 7s turn card potentially completes straight draws like 8-9, but the board is otherwise relatively dry. Folding to such a small bet is mathematically incorrect given the pot odds (3:1), and raising is risky as it likely forces out bluffs that I currently beat while isolating me against stronger hands. Calling is the most balanced approach to control the pot size and reach a showdown.

I am in the small blind holding a pair of Tens on a board of 6d 2h Ts 7s Qd. The Queen on the river is a dangerous card that likely improves the opponent's range, making my pair of Tens more of a bluff catcher than a value hand. Since there has been no betting on the river, checking is the most appropriate strategy to reach a cheap showdown and avoid bloating the pot against a potentially stronger hand.

After my turn semi-bluff was called, Flashlight has shown some resilience, suggesting he has a pair or a sticky draw. The Qd on the river could have easily connected with any overcards he might have been floating with, like KQ or QJ. Given my short stack, firing a second bluff here is too risky, as a call would severely cripple my remaining chips. Checking and conceding the pot is the safest and most prudent option.

I am holding a pocket pair of sevens on the button, which is a strong starting hand in this position. Raising here allows me to build the pot, potentially take down the blinds immediately, or play the hand with the advantage of position post-flop. A raise to 300 is a standard move to test the waters and gain initiative.

Holding 8h 4s in the small blind against a button raise is an easy fold. The hand has almost zero equity and playability, and with a short stack of 2550, I need to preserve chips for much better starting hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 4c 2c. Flashlight has raised to 3x from the button. While the hand is suited and I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot of 450), 4-2 suited is one of the weakest hands in the range. Playing this hand out of position against an active player like Flashlight is likely to lead to losing more chips post-flop. Given my strong chip lead and the proximity of the short-stacked 'Pro' to elimination, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand.

With 25.5 BBs, playing speculative hands like low suited one-gappers (64s) is risky. A raise could easily be met with a 3-bet or a call from the blinds, leaving me playing a weak hand post-flop with a stack size that doesn't offer great implied odds. Folding is the disciplined and optimal play here.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 4c, a weak hand. The pot is small, and I only have to call 50 more to see a flop. However, 8-4 offsuit is very low equity and generally not worth playing even for a discount, especially when Flashlight is in the Big Blind and has shown a willingness to be aggressive. Folding preserves my stack and keeps my range stronger for better opportunities.

I am on the button with King-Seven offsuit, which is a standard opening hand in 3-handed play. I have a significant chip lead, while the Big Blind (Pro) is quite short-stacked with only 24.5 big blinds. Raising to 300 allows me to apply pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. If either opponent defends, I have position for the rest of the hand.

I hold a very weak hand, 4-9 offsuit, and I am out of position in the small blind against a raise from the button. There is no incentive to call here with such a weak range, as playing out of position will be difficult and my hand has very low equity. Folding is the standard and most profitable play.

Facing a button open from Flash, I hold King-Queen suited in the big blind. With a stack of 24.5bb, a 3-bet to around 800 or 900 would commit a large portion of my stack and put me in an awkward spot if I miss the flop or get 4-bet jammed on. KQs plays excellently post-flop, so I will flat call to keep his wide opening range intact and evaluate on the flop.

With King-Queen on an Ace-Jack-Four flop, I have a gutshot to the broadway straight and a backdoor diamond draw. Since the board heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (especially with the Ace), the standard and most prudent play out of position is to check and evaluate his action.

On this Ace-high board, I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor from the button. Most of my opening range includes strong Aces and Jacks, while the Big Blind's calling range is often capped. Since Pro checked, a continuation bet is the most effective play to take down the pot immediately. A bet of 300 (roughly half the pot) is enough to force a fold from most of the Big Blind's non-pairing hands and draws, while keeping the risk manageable given Pro's relatively short stack.

Facing a continuation bet on an Ace-high board, my King-Queen high with a gutshot straight draw and backdoor flush draw is too weak to continue out of position. The pot odds do not justify calling with only four clean outs to the nuts, and my stack size of 22.5 big blinds makes floating unappealing. Folding is the most prudent decision to preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 9-high offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While the button offers positional advantage, the hand itself has very poor equity, and calling 100 chips to see a flop with such a dominated hand against two opponents is negative expected value. It is better to conserve my stack for stronger starting hands in better situations.

With K5s in the small blind and the button folded, this is a standard spot to attack the big blind. Raising to 300 puts pressure on Flash to defend, and we have a suited King which has decent equity and playability post-flop if we are called.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has folded, and the Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. Pro is quite short-stacked with only 1,950 chips remaining. In a blind-versus-blind situation, Ace-Nine is a very strong hand. Since I will have position on Pro for the remainder of the hand and the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 400, calling is the most standard play. This allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay the short stack post-flop without committing too much of my own stack pre-flop if he happens to have a monster hand.

The flop comes monotone with three spades, perfectly hitting the big blind's defending range, and I have absolutely no piece of it, lacking both a pair and a spade draw. Checking here is mandatory, as I have no equity to continue and a continuation bet is highly likely to be called or raised. I will easily fold if faced with a bet.

On this monotone flop of 4s-7s-8s, I have the Nine of spades, giving me a flush draw. The Small Blind (Pro) is short-stacked with only 1,950 chips remaining and has checked to me. While I could bet to try and take down the pot immediately, checking back allows me to take a free card in position to try and hit my flush or an Ace. This also avoids the risk of being check-raised by a short stack who might be looking to commit their remaining chips on a draw or a made hand. Checking is the safest way to realize my equity.

The turn brings the Jack of clubs, which does not improve my hand at all. I still only have King-high with no draw on a 3-spade board. Since I checked the flop and Flash checked behind, there is no reason to start bluffing now when I have zero equity. Checking to see a free river or folding to a bet is the only sensible option to preserve my short stack.

On the turn, the board is 4s-7s-8s-Jc. The Small Blind (Pro) has checked for a second time. I have the Nine of spades, providing a flush draw, and an overcard with the Ace. With Pro being short-stacked, checking back again is the most prudent play.
